preFix.title: Making a simple pine bookcase
preFix.subtitle: Cut and assemble a fixed-shelf bookcase from prepared timber
preFix.excerpt: How to build a basic freestanding bookcase from prepared timber: marking out the carcass, cutting the sides and shelves, joining with housing joints and wood screws, and finishing with sanding and a painted or oiled surface.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Carpentry for Boys (1914).

content.text: A simple bookcase requires only a handful of cuts and four basic joints. The method here uses prepared (planed, straight-edged) timber from a builders' merchant and wood screws through the side panels into the shelf ends, with PVA reinforcing each joint. The
goes together in an afternoon; the finish takes as long as it takes to dry.

Two side panels, the full height of the bookcase. Four or five shelves (including the top and bottom), each the width of the bookcase minus the thickness of both side panels. A 6 mm plywood or 3 mm hardboard back panel the full height and width. All timber: 19 mm prepared pine or MDF, 250 to 300 mm wide.

Mark all the shelf positions on both side panels simultaneously by clamping them face-to-face and marking across both at once. Use a combination square to mark lines exactly square across the face. Cut the shelves to length, checking each cut is square. Number each shelf and its matching position on the side panel in pencil on the back face so the layout does not get confused during assembly.
For fixed shelves, cut a stopped

in each side panel: a slot 6 mm deep and exactly as wide as the shelf thickness, stopping 25 mm short of the front edge. Use a router with a straight bit for precision, or cut the sides of the slot with a tenon saw and chisel out the waste. The shelf end notch fits the stopped end of the housing.

Dry-fit the entire carcass first to check all shelves fit square and flush. Disassemble. Apply PVA to all joint faces and reassemble. Clamp with sash cramps while the glue sets. Drive wood screws through the outer face of each side panel into the shelf end (pre-drill and countersink pilot holes to avoid splitting). Check the carcass is square by measuring the diagonals: they should be equal.

Cut the back panel to the full exterior dimensions of the carcass. Check it is square. Glue and pin with 20 mm panel pins, working from one corner outward. The back panel holds the carcass square; fitting it while the PVA at the joints is still tacky allows any racking to be corrected before the glue sets.

Sand all external faces through 120 and then 180 grit. Fill screw holes with wood filler, allow to dry, and re-sand. Paint with two coats of eggshell or apply an oil or wax finish to leave the pine grain visible.

attrs.fix: While the glue is still wet, push the long diagonal slightly with a clamp or sash cramp to pull it square. Pin the back panel while it is square to lock the dimension in.
attrs.cause: One shelf or joint is slightly out of alignment, or the side panels are not parallel.
attrs.symptom: Carcass not square when measured diagonally

attrs.fix: Plane the shelf end back slightly with a sharp block plane, working across the grain to avoid splitting.
attrs.cause: Housing depth slightly too shallow, or shelf end not cut square.
attrs.symptom: Shelves slightly proud of the side panel face

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
